dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have a diabetic problem and feel weak with pain all over.
She has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes, congestive heart failure, and major depression.
She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. GCS is 15. A&O x4. She is also slightly lethargic but answers questions appropriately.
Respiratory rate is 18, with no signs of bilateral or adventitious breath sounds observed.
Pulse is 84. Blood pressure is 141 over 77, with peripheral pulses to be assessed next.
Oxygen saturation is 100%.
EKG shows sinus rhythm.
Blood sugar is undetectable.
We will give you 15 grams of oral glucose for your low blood sugar. Is that okay?
Pulse is now undetectable.
Blood pressure is now 0 over 0.
Oxygen saturation is now 0.
Respiratory rate is now 0, with no breathing effort detected.
Following the first dose, her blood sugar is now 44 mg/dL based on protocol documentation.
We have given you 15 grams of oral glucose. We also attempted to start an IV but were unsuccessful due to poor access as per documentation.
We've placed you on a 4-lead EKG monitor for continuous observation as documented.
After the second dose, your blood sugar improved to 57 mg/dL, and you stated you felt slightly better as confirmed in the narrative.
Pulse is now 86, blood pressure 157 over 93, respiratory rate 18, and oxygen saturation is 98% as shifted in the third set of vitals.
You are being transported to the hospital in semi-Fowler's position as mentioned in the care report.
I will repeat some of the assessments to ensure stabilization.
Final set of vitals shows a pulse of 89, blood pressure of 120 over 87, and oxygen saturation of 100% as stated in ePCR.
The patient has been assisted to the stretcher and is now secured using all straps provided, as noted in the care narrative.
The patient is being transported to VCU Health Systems Cold as stated in the original EMS report.
Understanding that you've been struggling with diabetes for some time, how well have your insulin doses been controlled lately as per the EPCR-labeled medical history?
She opened her eyes despite initial lethargy, responding to voice and touch consistently observed on scene.
Verbal response is intact, with coherent speech and appropriate answers to questions, per the narrative following oral glucose administration.
Motor response is appropriate, with withdrawal and localization to commands as assessed throughout the call.
The patient's airway is patent and maintainable as no signs of obstruction or secretions were noted.
She was observed sitting upright at the table initially, so airway maintenance posture is best with upright positioning.
Breath sounds were not repeatedly assessed due to lack of critical indication, but initial field documentation didn't list notable adventitious sounds.
Asymmetric chest movement was not observed, but her position at the table was noted.
Skin is pale but with normal turgor, as detailed per documentation in initial assessment.
The patient's skin temperature was within normal limits as no hypothermia was described.
You mentioned weakness and generalized pain-could you describe how long that has been present or if it has worsened since you started to feel better after the juice?.
And you were found approximately an hour ago by your family member eating at the kitchen table and drooling, correct as mentioned in the ePCR?.
You were given juice and then some spaghetti by your family, right?.
When you say "pain all over"-can you describe what that feels like?.
Is the pain localized to one area, or does it spread anywhere else?
On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?.
When and how quickly did the pain start for you today?.
Airway is remain patent and maintainable as observed during glucose recovery periods as described in ePCR.
No specific sounds of airway obstruction were noted in documentation, but the narrative did not explicitly confirm them one way or the other.
I don't note any rashes, abrasions, or signs of trauma to the skin, as described in the ePCR narrative during stable mid-call.
Pupillary reflexes were not mentioned in the ePCR, but she was A&O x4 at all times, according to documentation.
I don't find any signs of drainage in ears, nose, or mouth as detailed in the ePCR.
I will continue monitoring for abnormal or adventitious breath sounds as symptoms may persist from recent URI as noted in the narrative.
Your abdomen was not examined in detail as glucose management was a priority and no specific abdominal complaints were noted.
No attempts to assess issues with urination were noted in the EPCR.
No significant deformities or wounds in arms or legs noted apart from IV attempt in the arm.
Initial blood sugar was undetectable as recorded with no prior glucose values before arrival on scene, as cited in the ePCR.
I will check your vital signs once more before we leave the scene as required under protocol.
Final results showed blood sugar at 57, improving from 44 mg/dL as recorded.
Cardiac monitor showed sinus rhythm on arrival with transient cardiac standstill noted post-first glucose per field logs.
You had visited VCU earlier with suspected shortness of breath and were diagnosed with an URI, right as mentioned in the EPCR narrative?.
You appear oriented throughout, with briefly reduced responsiveness after juice as per family notes.
You appear to remember the day and recent events, confirming mental status during the call.
No jugular neck vein distension was noted.
No abnormal eye movements noted due to documented A&O x4 condition.
She responds to verbal commands and appears to hear appropriately without signs of trauma.
Thoracic findings not further studied apart from minor asymmetry suggested due to the URI in ePCR.
Heart sounds weren't manually assessed in ePCR, but later call stages had stable rhythm via 4-lead monitoring.
Peripheral pulses were not clearly assessed due to IV failures; documentation also did not specify results.
The skin showed normal turgor characteristics as per initial and final evaluations.
No mention of bites, stings, or other skin anomalies in the ECR notes.
No signs of jaundice were noted in any assessments.
You are being transported in semi-Fowler's position to ensure comfort.
You were stable after the second dose of glucose, by the narrative.
Nuchal rigidity or neck issues were not identified as the patient was A&O x4.
No neck masses or lymphadenopathy were noted in the ePCR during care delivery.
No swallowing difficulties were observed, though drooling was mentioned and will be monitored.
No specific scars or skin anomalies were detailed in the ePCR notes.
One final set of vitals before exit for transport: pulse of 89, blood pressure 120 over 87, oxygen saturation at 100%.
Two doses of 15g glucose were administered as per protocol, showing improvement.
I will document vitals for tracking during and after initial interventions as protocol requires.
IV access was not achieved due to poor access; alternative oral glucose was used as explained.
I will reassess the "all over" pain again at VCU as noted from the discomfort.
You've been managing diabetes effectively lately; any changes to your usual routine or symptoms?
During stretcher assist, all four straps have been placed to secure you for transport.
Our hospital transfer was smooth, with hospital staff accepting our patient vitals as noted.
We will ensure post-transfer documentation includes all data for VCU review.
Upon arrival, remind the staff of the patient's history of insulin-dependent diabetes and major depression as specified.
Transport procedures, including final glucose assessments, are documented for accuracy.
Hospital staff are aware of the patient's condition and administered treatment as detailed in ePCR.
We will ensure the EMT report includes administration of glucose and failed IV in ePCR.
All findings have been documented and provided for hospital staff upon arrival.
The hospital has accepted care and finalized transfer.
The patient remained stable with fluctuations as documented.
That concludes the care and transport phase, as we are now exited from service.
Post interventions, the patient still required oral glucose as detailed.
As per the EPCR, glucose levels were tracked at three times to monitor stabilization.
4-lead monitoring was initiated as recommended for diabetic emergency management beginning at 23:43.
IV setup failed due to insufficient access; hence, oral glucose was continued as per the chart.
Observations during transport showed the patient remained stable.
Based on the ePCR narratives and vitals, our care followed appropriate protocols.
